nativeRange.habitat: Subtidal reef flats and protected lagoons over rubble-algal substrate, typically at depths of 1–20 m

decorPreferences: sandy or fine coralline gravel substrate

diet.notes: Has been documented eating benthic invertebrates and some algae in the wild (trophic level ~3.5). In captivity tends to accept a varied diet of meaty marine foods including squid, krill, clams, mussels, hard-shelled shrimp, and chunks of fish. Offering hard-shelled foods is generally advised to help wear down the species' continuously growing teeth.

goodWith: tangs of similar or larger size

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe; 2087x1565. Tack-sharp full-body in-situ shot showing the diagnostic large black belly patch, half-white/half-yellow face and blue accent lines on a vibrant live individual.
Rickard Zerpe; 1644x1233. Sharp three-quarter view; clearly shows black belly patch, red-lined mouth and blue/yellow face markings for a different angle.
desertnaturalist; 1024x563. Sharp wild in-situ side profile of a different individual showing yellow/blue face lines, red mouth and caudal-peduncle bands.

summary: An Indo-West Pacific triggerfish marked by a distinctive black belly patch and a half-white, half-yellow face; widely considered hardy in captivity but aggressive and not reef-safe.
careNotes:
The Bursa Triggerfish (also sold as the Blackbelly or Black Patch Triggerfish) is generally considered one of the hardier triggerfish species for fish-only-with-live-rock (FOWLR — a reef-style setup without living corals) aquariums, but it is decidedly not a community or reef fish. Adults can be shy initially but tend to become territorial and highly aggressive toward conspecifics, other triggerfishes, and any similarly shaped or colored tankmates. The species has been known to consume ornamental shrimp, snails, clams, and most other invertebrates, and may damage corals and sessile life, so it is generally not considered reef-safe.

The Bursa Triggerfish commonly rearranges live rock and sand and has been reported to make audible grunting vocalizations when threatened or feeding. Aquarists have also reported individuals biting plastic, silicone, and aquarium equipment; secure, well-anchored rockwork is generally advised. Like other members of the triggerfish family (Balistidae), it has strong, continuously growing teeth that benefit from regular hard-shelled prey such as whole clams and shell-on shrimp. Adults reach around 19–25 cm and can be long-lived, with reports of 15–20 years in well-maintained aquariums.
breedingNotes: Oviparous with distinct pairing in the wild; in the wild has been reported to spawn in loose aggregations or as mated pairs over rubble-algal substrate. Captive breeding has not been meaningfully documented in the aquarium literature and is generally regarded as very difficult outside large public-aquarium settings.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Collection page used as entry; individual product page (liveaquaria.com/products/bursa-triggerfish) provided the bulk of the husbandry values (130 gal min tank, care level easy, temperament aggressive, reef-safe no, temperature 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, carnivore diet, 9-inch adult size).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Authoritative taxonomy (Balistidae, Tetraodontiformes), maximum size (23 cm TL, common 19 cm), wild temperature range (26.2–29.3°C), Indo-West Pacific distribution (Seychelles/Chagos to Vanuatu, south Japan), depth range 1–20 m, oviparous with distinct pairing, IUCN Least Concern. Diet listed as benthic invertebrates and algae at trophic level ~3.5.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (23 cm), reef-safety (no), temperament (aggressive), and tank-size recommendation (1,000 L). Notes on rock-rearranging behavior, biting plastic/rubber, and grunting vocalizations. ReefApp's 1,000 L value sits at the high end of the source spread but matched the prose-context cues (territorial, needs space).

additional.name: FreshMarine
additional.notes: Cross-checked water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025), carnivore diet, reef-safe no, easy care level. Tank size (75 gal / 284 L) was on the low end of the spread; included in the average but biased away from for the final value.

additional.name: Tropical Fish Keeping (search result)
additional.notes: WebFetch returned HTTP 429; data extracted via WebSearch snippet. Confirmed 125 gal (~473 L) minimum tank, water conditions 72–78°F / dKH 8–12 / pH 8.1–8.4 / SG 1.020–1.023, 9-inch adult size, lifespan up to ~20 years, aggressive temperament, carnivore diet, not reef-safe.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous with distinct pairing in the wild; not meaningfully bred in home aquaria.
